label:
SES-071 PI-045 V2 remote-access deployment — operational steps 1 through 4 executed at Doug's Linux Mint 22 laptop and the Cloudflare dashboard. Domain crmbuilder.ai's nameservers migrated from GoDaddy to Cloudflare (free plan, team name 'crmbuilder', team_domain crmbuilder.cloudflareaccess.com) with all 5 Google Workspace MX records plus all 5 email-related TXT records (apex SPF, delegated SPF subdomain dc-aa8e722993._spfm, google._domainkey DKIM, _dmarc, google-site-verification) preserved through the migration; the delegated SPF subdomain was missed by Cloudflare's initial DNS scan and added manually. cloudflared 2026.5.0 installed via Cloudflare's apt repository on the Mint-22 host (Ubuntu noble base — codename substitution required since pkg.cloudflare.com lacks zara distribution); zone certificate obtained via cloudflared tunnel login. Tunnel crmbuilder-mcp (UUID 6170403a-eb17-47ee-af94-ea70de6a1e74) created with DNS CNAME mcp.crmbuilder.ai → <UUID>.cfargotunnel.com and config.yml ingress rule routing to 127.0.0.1:8810 plus 404 catch-all. Systemd user unit at ~/.config/systemd/user/crmbuilder-mcp-tunnel.service with lingering enabled via sudo loginctl enable-linger; tunnel persists across reboot and login session boundaries. Cloudflare Access protects mcp.crmbuilder.ai with a Self-hosted Access Application 'CRM Builder MCP' using One-time PIN identity provider, gated by two policies: 'Service Auth claude-ai-mcp' (Action=Service Auth, Include=Service Token claude-ai-mcp) for programmatic claude.ai traffic and 'Allow admin' (Action=Allow, Include=Emails=admin@crmbuilder.ai) for browser admin. DEC-225 settles the Cloudflare Access auth model revisions (Service Token added to satisfy programmatic clients that cannot follow OAuth redirects; One-time PIN replaces Google login because Cloudflare retired the built-in Google OAuth shortcut and bring-your-own Google Cloud OAuth setup is meaningful overhead for a low-frequency single-user admin path). All three auth paths verified end-to-end: unauthenticated requests return HTTP 302 redirecting to crmbuilder.cloudflareaccess.com login; Service Token requests bearing CF-Access-Client-Id and CF-Access-Client-Secret headers return HTTP 502 (auth passes, tunnel reaches 127.0.0.1:8810, no MCP backend listening yet); browser requests authenticated via One-time PIN to admin@crmbuilder.ai reach origin and return 502 from the same tunnel-to-empty-backend path. Step 5 (registering the MCP URL in claude.ai's MCP integration with the X-CRMBuilder-Secret app middleware header and the two CF-Access-* service-token headers) is gated on the PI-045 code-changes implementation conversation completing — slices A (CLI transport flag + FastMCP HTTP binding), B (X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware), and C (engagement-marker fail-loud guard per DEC-205) have not yet been applied. Identifier rebase note: this work was initially scoped as SES-069 / DEC-216 per the PI-045 kickoff's anticipated identifiers. Three parallel-sandbox commits between this conversation's start and end advanced the identifier heads: SES-069 / DEC-216–220 / PI-048 (PI-023 reconciliation utility planning, applied at commit 1435f0a), SES-070 / DEC-221–224 (PI-030 architecture planning, payload committed at 232e967 awaiting apply), and a 'PI-022 governance-backfill program closed' commit (b34391f). Rebased to SES-071 / DEC-225 to avoid collision. The four parallel-sandbox occurrences flagged in the kickoff (SES-057→058, SES-058→059, SES-062→063→064) and the further SES-068 occurrence now extend to a fifth, which strengthens the PI-032 case for reserve-at-apply-time identifier model.

1 session, 1 decision, 0 planning items, 3 references.

session.title: PI-045 V2 remote-access deployment — operational steps 1 through 4 (nameserver migration to Cloudflare with Google Workspace email preserved; cloudflared installed and tunnel crmbuilder-mcp running as systemd user service routing mcp.crmbuilder.ai to 127.0.0.1:8810; Cloudflare Access two-policy split — Service Auth for claude-ai-mcp Service Token and Allow + One-time PIN for admin browser path); DEC-225 settles the auth model revisions (Service Token added for programmatic clients, One-time PIN replaces Google login since Cloudflare retired the built-in Google OAuth shortcut)
session.conversation_reference: Claude.ai conversation opened against the seed prompt 'Open the PI-045 operational deployment conversation. Read the kickoff at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-045-remote-access-deployment-kickoff.md and proceed with part (b).' Operating mode: ARCHITECTURE-default per the kickoff's working-pattern section, with DETAIL-flavor procedural walkthrough for the step-by-step ops itself. The conversation executed operational steps 1 through 4 of PI-045's five-step operational scope; step 5 (registering the MCP URL in claude.ai's MCP integration) is gated on the PI-045 code-changes conversation (slices A/B/C) completing first and is deferred. Orientation read: crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md (sparse-checkout pattern, v0.7 governance entity state, sandbox commit-and-push convention, vocab.py reference relationship rules); the PI-045 kickoff end-to-end including the surface-and-settle decision on engagement-marker drift and the five decide-and-announce items settled in SES-065; the SES-065 close-out payload at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_065.json for the parameters this conversation operates against (port 8810, header X-CRMBuilder-Secret, secret storage at ~/.config/crmbuilder/mcp-secret with launchd-plist sourcing, Cloudflare Access Google login, documentation home at crmbuilder-v2/docs/remote-deployment.md); the four SES-064 anchor decisions DEC-201 through DEC-204 by title via the db-export decisions snapshot. The sandbox initially saw identifier heads at SES-068 / DEC-215 / PI-047 / COP-069 / DEP-048 before opening Step 1; by the close-out authoring step, a second git fetch revealed three parallel-sandbox commits had landed during the conversation: SES-069 with DEC-216 through DEC-220 and PI-048 (PI-023 reconciliation utility planning), SES-070 with DEC-221 through DEC-224 awaiting apply (PI-030 architecture planning), and 'PI-022 governance-backfill program closed' status flip on PI-022. The conversation rebased its anticipated SES-069 / DEC-216 to SES-071 / DEC-225 — fifth parallel-sandbox identifier-rebase occurrence in the recent program, reinforcing the PI-032 case for reserve-at-apply-time identifiers but not blocking this conversation. Doug ran every executable step at his terminal (Linux Mint 22, Ubuntu noble base, hostname doug-System-Product-Name) and the Cloudflare dashboard at dash.cloudflare.com/one and pasted command outputs and screenshots back; Claude provided UI-level navigation and bash command blocks, diagnosed each verification failure, and corrected procedure when Cloudflare's UI organization differed from the kickoff's assumptions. The conversation closes with two files committed and pushed together in one commit per the sandbox push convention: this close-out payload at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_071.json and the SES-071 apply prompt at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-071.md.
session.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Open the PI-045 operational deployment conversation. Read the kickoff at `PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/pi-045-remote-access-deployment-kickoff.md` and proceed with part (b)."

Structured summary of the operational conversation:

(1) Orientation and reframing. Read crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md and the PI-045 kickoff. Verified status of PI-045's anchor decisions DEC-201..204 via the db-export decisions snapshot. Confirmed the planning conversation SES-065 had applied (DEC-205 fail-loud on marker drift; five decide-and-announce items settled — MCP HTTP port 8810, header X-CRMBuilder-Secret, secret storage at ~/.config/crmbuilder/mcp-secret with launchd-plist sourcing, Cloudflare Access Google login matching Doug's owner identity, documentation home at crmbuilder-v2/docs/remote-deployment.md). Confirmed PI-045 status was 'Open' — code-changes conversation (slices A/B/C) not yet applied. Surfaced the sequencing observation to Doug: operational steps 1–4 are infrastructure-independent of MCP code and can proceed without it being live; step 5 (claude.ai MCP integration registration) is gated on the HTTP transport actually being up. Doug approved 'proceed with steps 1–4, gate step 5.'

(2) Two clerical clarifications surfaced during orientation. The kickoff and SES-065 throughout name the domain as 'crmbuilder.com' but Doug's actual domain is 'crmbuilder.ai' (singular, no 's' — initial pasted-back 'crmbuilders.ai' was a typo that got reconciled to crmbuilder.ai across this session). The hostname under DEC-204 thus becomes 'mcp.crmbuilder.ai' instead of 'mcp.crmbuilder.com'. Clerical drift, no architectural change — DEC-204's substantive decision (Cloudflare Tunnel + Cloudflare Access identity gating on a single subdomain under Doug's zone) is preserved unchanged. Separately, the kickoff and SES-065 specified launchd-plist for the service manager assuming macOS, but Doug's machine is Linux Mint 22 — substituted systemd user units throughout. Both clarifications are captured in this session's narrative rather than as supersedes edges since they don't change any settled architectural decision.

(3) Step 1 — nameserver migration crmbuilder.ai GoDaddy → Cloudflare. Cloudflare zone added at dash.cloudflare.com with team name 'crmbuilder' (replacing the auto-assigned 'summer-scene-4e25'), team_domain crmbuilder.cloudflareaccess.com, free plan. Cloudflare's DNS scan auto-imported 13 records; reviewed each row against the GoDaddy ground-truth panel. Deleted 4 GoDaddy-default records (2 A records to GoDaddy parking IPs 15.197.148.33 and 3.33.130.190; the cname _domainconnect → _domainconnect.gd.domaincontrol.com which is GoDaddy's auto-DNS handshake protocol; the cname www → crmbuilder.ai which pointed to the parking apex). Kept all 10 substantive records (5 Google Workspace MX records aspmx.l.google.com and alt1–4 with correct priorities; TXT google-site-verification for Google Workspace ownership; apex TXT v=spf1 include:dc-aa8e722993._spfm.crmbuilder.ai ~all; TXT google._domainkey for DKIM signature; TXT _dmarc v=DMARC1 p=quarantine). Cloudflare's scanner missed the delegated SPF subdomain TXT at dc-aa8e722993._spfm — added manually via the Add Record dialog with type=TXT, name=dc-aa8e722993._spfm, content=v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com ~all (without this record, outbound Google Workspace mail would have started failing SPF validation as a void lookup after nameserver flip). At GoDaddy, switched custom nameservers to grant.ns.cloudflare.com and luciane.ns.cloudflare.com. Cloudflare dashboard initially showed 'Invalid nameservers' (timing/staleness; whois confirmed both Cloudflare nameservers at the .ai registry; resolved within ~30 minutes when Cloudflare's recheck ran). Verified email records survived migration with dig commands at the local resolver after Cloudflare reported zone Active: NS resolves to both Cloudflare nameservers, all 5 MX records present, apex SPF + Google-site-verification TXTs present, the manually-added dc-aa8e722993._spfm TXT resolves correctly, google._domainkey DKIM resolves with the full RSA public key (two-string split per DNS protocol for keys >255 chars), _dmarc TXT resolves with the v=DMARC1 policy intact. Step 1 closed.

(4) Step 2 — install cloudflared and authenticate. Initial apt install via the pkg.cloudflare.com repo failed because lsb_release -cs returned 'zara' (the Linux Mint 22 codename) and Cloudflare's apt repo is keyed on upstream Ubuntu codenames — overwrote /etc/apt/sources.list.d/cloudflared.list to hardcode 'noble' (Mint 22's Ubuntu base) and reran apt update + install. cloudflared 2026.5.0 installed cleanly at /usr/local/bin/cloudflared (and also /usr/bin/cloudflared; both paths point to identical binaries from the same deb install). Pre-existing VirtualBox 7.0.16 DKMS failure against kernel 6.17 surfaced in the apt output as 'dpkg returned an error code (1)' but is unrelated to cloudflared and was already pending from prior apt state — flagged for separate cleanup, out of PI-045 scope. cloudflared tunnel login opened a browser to Cloudflare, Doug selected the crmbuilder.ai zone and authorized, the zone certificate was saved to ~/.cloudflared/cert.pem (mode 0600, ~5–10 KB). Step 2 closed.

(5) Step 3 — tunnel + DNS + systemd user service. Created the named tunnel 'crmbuilder-mcp' (UUID 6170403a-eb17-47ee-af94-ea70de6a1e74) via cloudflared tunnel create; tunnel credentials written to ~/.cloudflared/6170403a-eb17-47ee-af94-ea70de6a1e74.json. Created the public DNS route via cloudflared tunnel route dns crmbuilder-mcp mcp.crmbuilder.ai — Cloudflare added the CNAME mcp.crmbuilder.ai → <UUID>.cfargotunnel.com. Wrote ~/.cloudflared/config.yml with tunnel + credentials-file + ingress rule (hostname mcp.crmbuilder.ai → service http://127.0.0.1:8810) and the required terminal http_status:404 catch-all. Foreground test (cloudflared tunnel run crmbuilder-mcp) registered four Cloudflare edge connections; external curl https://mcp.crmbuilder.ai through cloudflared's edge cert (Let's Encrypt wildcard *.crmbuilder.ai, the cert that Cloudflare provisioned automatically on the zone) returned HTTP 502 via 172.67.219.60 as expected (tunnel routes correctly, no origin listening on :8810). Wrapped under systemd user service at ~/.config/systemd/user/crmbuilder-mcp-tunnel.service with ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/cloudflared --no-autoupdate --config /home/doug/.cloudflared/config.yml tunnel run crmbuilder-mcp, Restart=on-failure, RestartSec=5s, WantedBy=default.target. sudo loginctl enable-linger doug enables the service to keep running without an active login session. systemctl --user daemon-reload + enable + start brought the service to 'Active: active (running)', and a re-run of the curl test under systemd-managed cloudflared returned the same HTTP 502 via Cloudflare edge IP. Two log warnings noted as non-blocking and unrelated to HTTP tunneling: ICMP proxy disabled because GID 1000 is not in ping_group_range (cloudflared can optionally proxy ICMP through tunnels but this feature is unused for HTTP-only routing); QUIC receive buffer 'failed to sufficiently increase' from kernel default 208 KiB to cloudflared's preferred 7168 KiB (got 416 KiB; affects throughput on high-volume QUIC flows, not low-volume MCP traffic; tunable via net.core.rmem_max if it ever matters). Step 3 closed.

(6) Step 4 — Cloudflare Access setup; two consequential decisions surfaced inline, captured as DEC-225. First surfaced decision (option A vs B vs C in the 'Surface and settle' on Cloudflare Access for programmatic clients): SES-065's settled 'Google login (Doug's identity)' assumed a browser-only client and would 302-redirect claude.ai's MCP integration on every request (claude.ai cannot follow OAuth redirects). Three options framed — single Access app with two Include rules under one Allow policy; two Access applications under different hostnames; or drop Cloudflare Access entirely and rely solely on the shared-secret middleware. Recommended Option A (preserves DEC-204's dual-layer security model with minimum complexity); Doug approved 'a'. Second surfaced decision: in the middle of configuring the Google IdP, Cloudflare's UI required an App ID — Cloudflare has retired the built-in Google OAuth shortcut and now requires bring-your-own Google Cloud OAuth credentials for the Google IdP. The 'use Cloudflare's built-in Google OAuth credentials' half of SES-065's decide-and-announce on Google login was thus no longer viable as written. Three replacement options framed — set up Doug's own Google OAuth (Google Cloud project, consent screen, credentials, ~15 min upfront and ongoing project maintenance); use Cloudflare's already-enabled One-time PIN identity provider (no setup, per-login PIN-check friction); or any other federated IdP (similar third-party setup overhead to Google). Recommended Option B (One-time PIN for low-frequency single-user admin path; zero setup beats ongoing Google Cloud project management; claude.ai's path is the actually-important one and is handled by the Service Token from Option A above). Doug approved 'approve'. Both auth-model revisions consolidated into DEC-225 with a references edge to DEC-204.

(7) Step 4 implementation. Substantial Cloudflare-One UI navigation friction was encountered because Cloudflare reorganized Zero Trust into Cloudflare One since the kickoff was authored: the old 'Settings → Authentication → Login methods' path no longer exists; identity providers now live at 'Integrations → Identity providers' (the renamed Login Methods page) and service tokens at 'Access controls → Service credentials → Service Tokens'. Created service token 'claude-ai-mcp' with non-expiring duration; captured Client ID + Client Secret from the one-time modal. Initial credential-file write polluted both ~/.config/crmbuilder/cf-access-client-id and ~/.config/crmbuilder/cf-access-client-secret with the header-name prefix from copy-paste ('CF-Access-Client-Id: 39ac09…' instead of just '39ac09…'); the doubled header-name caused early verbose-curl-grep output to show '> CF-Access-Client-Id: CF-Access-Client-Id: 39ac09d4d7c707c75c2b9187a896d67c.access'. Stripped the prefix in place using tail -c +22 / +26 to avoid re-pasting (which would have risked re-introducing the same error). Result: ~/.config/crmbuilder/cf-access-client-id = 39 bytes ending in '.access', cf-access-client-secret = 64 hex bytes, both mode 0600. Hygiene followup: the Client Secret value appeared in conversation transcript during the diagnostic; blast radius is currently zero (no MCP backend listening, no shared-secret middleware yet), but rotation is required before step 5 / claude.ai integration goes live — captured in in_flight_at_end.

(8) Created Self-hosted Access Application 'CRM Builder MCP' protecting subdomain=mcp, domain=crmbuilder.ai, path=blank (matches all paths). Application uses One-time PIN as identity provider with 'Accept all available identity providers' toggle on (effectively OTP-only since OTP is the only enabled IdP). Created two Access policies. Policy 'Allow admin' — Action=Allow, Include rule Selector=Emails Value=admin@crmbuilder.ai, session duration 24 hours. Policy 'Service Auth claude-ai-mcp' — Action=Service Auth, Include rule Selector=Service Token Value=claude-ai-mcp. Both attached to the CRM Builder MCP application via the application's Access policies section. The two-policy split is essential — initial single-policy attempt with both Include rules under Allow returned HTTP 302 on service-token requests because Cloudflare honors service-token validation only under Action=Service Auth (or via a separate Service Auth policy attached to the same app), not as an Include rule under Allow. Diagnostic chain: verbose curl confirmed the CF-Access-Client-Id and CF-Access-Client-Secret headers were being sent correctly; the JWT meta in the 302's Location header showed 'service_token_status: false, auth_status: NONE' which means Cloudflare's edge was not validating the token; root cause traced to the Service Auth policy existing in the global Policies library but never having been attached to the application (Cloudflare's app-creation wizard creates an inline policy but does not auto-attach external policies created later — they must be added via the application's 'Add existing policy' control). After explicit attachment, the application's Access policies section showed both 'Allow admin' (Action=ALLOW) and 'Service Auth claude-ai-mcp' (Action=SERVICE AUTH); service-token requests began returning HTTP 502 from the tunnel as expected.

(9) Three auth paths verified end-to-end. Path 1 (no auth): HTTP 302 to https://crmbuilder.cloudflareaccess.com/cdn-cgi/access/login/mcp.crmbuilder.ai with JWT meta showing auth_status=NONE; confirms Access policy is enforced. Path 2 (Service Token): HTTP 502 with curl bearing CF-Access-Client-Id and CF-Access-Client-Secret headers from the credential files; the 502 is from the origin layer through the tunnel since 127.0.0.1:8810 is not listening yet; confirms claude.ai's path will work once the MCP HTTP transport from PI-045 slice A is up. Path 3 (browser + OTP): visited https://mcp.crmbuilder.ai in a browser, entered admin@crmbuilder.ai at the Cloudflare Access login page, received the 6-digit One-time PIN by email, pasted into the browser, was redirected to the origin and saw the Cloudflare 502 error page; confirms browser admin path is functional. Step 4 closed. PI-045 operational steps 1–4 complete.

(10) Identifier rebase. At conversation start the heads were SES-068 / DEC-215 / PI-047. By close-out time, three parallel-sandbox commits had landed: 1435f0a 'Apply SES-069 close-out: PI-023 reconciliation utility planning — DEC-216..220 and PI-048', 232e967 'PI-030 architecture planning close-out (SES-070) — payload, apply prompt, and three slice prompts' (payload file present, apply not yet run), and b34391f 'PI-022 governance-backfill program closed — PI-022 status Open -> Resolved'. Heads in the snapshot at close-out time: SES-069, DEC-220, PI-048 in the applied set; SES-070, DEC-221–224 claimed in the unapplied repo file. This conversation rebased its anticipated SES-069 / DEC-216 to SES-071 / DEC-225 — first rebase to skip an unapplied-but-claimed identifier (SES-070), fifth occurrence of the parallel-sandbox pattern in the recent program. The apply prompt for this conversation accommodates two head states pre-apply (either SES-069/DEC-220 if SES-070 has not yet been applied, or SES-070/DEC-224 if it has).

(11) Two files committed and pushed together at close-out per sandbox convention: this payload at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/close-out-payloads/ses_071.json and the apply prompt at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-071.md.
session.artifacts_produced: On Doug's Linux Mint 22 host: ~/.cloudflared/cert.pem (zone certificate from cloudflared tunnel login, mode 0600); ~/.cloudflared/6170403a-eb17-47ee-af94-ea70de6a1e74.json (tunnel credentials); ~/.cloudflared/config.yml (ingress rules); ~/.config/crmbuilder/cf-access-client-id (39 bytes, mode 0600, Service Token Client ID for claude-ai-mcp); ~/.config/crmbuilder/cf-access-client-secret (64 bytes, mode 0600, Service Token Client Secret); ~/.config/systemd/user/crmbuilder-mcp-tunnel.service (systemd user unit running cloudflared with --no-autoupdate against the config.yml ingress); systemd user-lingering enabled for user 'doug' so the tunnel persists without an active login session. On Cloudflare's side: zone crmbuilder.ai on the free plan with team name 'crmbuilder' and team_domain crmbuilder.cloudflareaccess.com; 10 DNS records (5 Google Workspace MX + 5 email-related TXT including the manually-added dc-aa8e722993._spfm delegated SPF subdomain that Cloudflare's auto-scan missed); cloudflared tunnel 'crmbuilder-mcp' UUID 6170403a-eb17-47ee-af94-ea70de6a1e74 with DNS CNAME mcp.crmbuilder.ai → <UUID>.cfargotunnel.com; One-time PIN identity provider enabled at Integrations → Identity providers; Service Token 'claude-ai-mcp' (non-expiring); Self-hosted Access Application 'CRM Builder MCP' for mcp.crmbuilder.ai with One-time PIN IdP; two Access policies attached — 'Allow admin' (Action=Allow, Include=Emails=admin@crmbuilder.ai) and 'Service Auth claude-ai-mcp' (Action=Service Auth, Include=Service Token=claude-ai-mcp). In the crmbuilder repo: this close-out payload and the SES-071 apply prompt.
session.in_flight_at_end: Step 5 of PI-045 (register the MCP URL https://mcp.crmbuilder.ai in claude.ai's MCP integration with the X-CRMBuilder-Secret custom header and the two CF-Access-Client-Id / CF-Access-Client-Secret service-token headers) is gated on the PI-045 code-changes implementation conversation completing first. That conversation runs slices A (--transport stdio/streamable-http flag plus FastMCP HTTP binding to 127.0.0.1:8810), B (X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware), and C (engagement-marker fail-loud guard per DEC-205) in sequence at Doug's terminal via Claude Code. Once slice C lands and the MCP HTTP transport is running, step 5 can be executed in a short follow-up operational conversation or folded into the smoke-test conversation. Hygiene followup before step 5 goes live: rotate the claude-ai-mcp Service Token's Client Secret — the secret value appeared in this conversation's transcript during the diagnostic of the doubled-header bug. Blast radius is currently zero (no MCP backend listening, no shared-secret middleware yet to gate), but the value should not survive into the operational MCP traffic window. Rotation is one round-trip in the Cloudflare dashboard: Cloudflare Zero Trust → Access controls → Service credentials → Service Tokens → claude-ai-mcp → Refresh; capture new Client ID + Client Secret from the one-time modal atomically with printf '%s' '<value>' > ~/.config/crmbuilder/cf-access-client-{id,secret} (no trailing newline, no header-prefix); update canonical copy in Doug's password manager. Also pending application of the SES-070 close-out (PI-030 architecture planning, DEC-221–224) — apply order between SES-070 and SES-071 is unconstrained; both can apply in either order without collision since their identifier spaces are disjoint.

decisions.title: Cloudflare Access auth model revisions for PI-045: Service Token added for programmatic clients; One-time PIN replaces Google login for browser admin (Cloudflare retired the built-in Google OAuth shortcut)
decisions.context: DEC-204 settled the v2 remote-access architecture's outer security layer as 'Cloudflare Tunnel on a single subdomain under Doug's zone, gated by Cloudflare Access identity gating' — a dual-layer model alongside the inner X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware (from PI-045 slice B). SES-065's decide-and-announce items elaborated the IdP choice as 'Google login (matches Doug's primary identity), single Access policy on the hostname requiring Google auth for Doug's owner email,' built on the implicit assumption that Cloudflare's built-in Google OAuth shortcut was available (no Google Cloud project setup required) and that a single Allow policy with Email+ServiceToken Includes would suffice for both authentication paths. Operational implementation of PI-045 step 4 surfaced two gaps that the planning conversation did not grapple with. First gap: claude.ai's MCP integration is a backend HTTP client with no browser, no cookie store, and no OAuth-redirect-following capability. A Google-login-only Access policy would 302-redirect every claude.ai request to https://crmbuilder.cloudflareaccess.com/cdn-cgi/access/login/... which the MCP integration cannot follow; claude.ai would be dead at the Access edge before reaching the tunnel, the X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware, or the MCP server. Cloudflare ships a purpose-built primitive for exactly this scenario — Service Tokens, a Client ID + Client Secret pair sent via CF-Access-Client-Id and CF-Access-Client-Secret request headers — which Access validates without redirects. Second gap: Cloudflare retired the built-in Google OAuth shortcut. The Google IdP setup form now requires bring-your-own Google Cloud OAuth credentials (Google Cloud project, OAuth consent screen, OAuth Client ID + Secret); the 'leave Client ID and Client Secret blank for built-in' path described in older Cloudflare documentation no longer applies. This materially changes the cost-benefit of Google vs One-time PIN for the browser-admin path: where SES-065 framed Google as 'zero setup' it is now '~15 min initial setup plus ongoing Google Cloud project maintenance' for a single-user low-frequency admin path.
decisions.decision: Cloudflare Access protecting mcp.crmbuilder.ai uses TWO policies attached to the same Self-hosted Access Application 'CRM Builder MCP'. Policy 1 'Service Auth claude-ai-mcp' has Action=Service Auth and a single Include rule Selector=Service Token Value=claude-ai-mcp; this is the programmatic-client path that claude.ai exercises. Policy 2 'Allow admin' has Action=Allow and a single Include rule Selector=Emails Value=admin@crmbuilder.ai over the One-time PIN identity provider (Cloudflare emails a 6-digit PIN to admin@crmbuilder.ai which the browser admin pastes back); this is the manual browser-admin path Doug exercises for ad-hoc inspection. Cloudflare Access evaluates Service Auth policies first and Allow policies afterward per Cloudflare's documented semantics, so policy order is informational only. claude.ai's MCP integration sends three custom headers per request: CF-Access-Client-Id (the Service Token's Client ID), CF-Access-Client-Secret (the Service Token's Client Secret), and X-CRMBuilder-Secret (the inner shared-secret from SES-065 slice B). Service Token credentials are stored at ~/.config/crmbuilder/cf-access-client-id and ~/.config/crmbuilder/cf-access-client-secret (mode 0600 each, no trailing newline, no header-name prefix); canonical copies live in Doug's password manager out-of-band; rotation is a manual five-place edit (the two credential files, the canonical password-manager entries, claude.ai's MCP integration's custom header configuration for both CF-Access-* headers, and the Service Token refresh action in Cloudflare's UI).
decisions.rationale: Preserves DEC-204's dual-layer model verbatim — Cloudflare Access remains the outer authentication gate; the X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware remains the inner application gate; together they constitute the dual layer DEC-204 named. Service Tokens are Cloudflare's purpose-built primitive for programmatic clients behind Access, removing the need for any workaround. One-time PIN preserves the spirit of SES-065's 'authenticate Doug for browser admin' goal while eliminating Google Cloud project setup and ongoing maintenance for a low-frequency single-user path; the per-login PIN-check friction (email arrives, copy PIN, paste) is acceptable for the actual usage pattern (occasional ad-hoc browser admin, not daily logins).
decisions.alternatives_considered: For the programmatic-client path: (a) two separate Access applications under different hostnames (e.g. claude.mcp.crmbuilder.ai for Service Token, admin.mcp.crmbuilder.ai for Google login) — more moving pieces (two DNS records, two ingress rules, two Access apps, two policies) with no functional benefit when there is only one programmatic consumer; (b) drop Cloudflare Access entirely for the hostname and rely solely on the X-CRMBuilder-Secret middleware — contradicts DEC-204's identity-gating half and reduces the security model from dual-layer to single-layer. For the browser-admin path: (a) bring-your-own Google OAuth credentials — adds a Google Cloud project to Doug's surface, ~15 min initial setup, and ongoing OAuth-app maintenance (rotation, consent-screen updates, possible OAuth verification process) for a low-frequency admin path; (b) any other federated IdP (Microsoft, GitHub, Okta, OneLogin) — same third-party-setup overhead as Google, no compensating benefit.
decisions.consequences: claude.ai's MCP integration must be configured with three custom headers (CF-Access-Client-Id, CF-Access-Client-Secret, X-CRMBuilder-Secret) — Step 5 of PI-045 captures this. Rotation surface grows to five touchpoints (the two credential files at ~/.config/crmbuilder/, the password-manager canonical entries, claude.ai's MCP integration's three header values, and the Service Token refresh action in Cloudflare's UI plus the corresponding shared-secret rotation in the launchd/systemd unit's environment for X-CRMBuilder-Secret). The Service Token's Client Secret was exposed in this conversation's transcript during a doubled-header diagnostic — captured in this session's in_flight_at_end as a pre-step-5 hygiene rotation. Cloudflare Access's policy evaluation rule (Service Auth and Bypass policies evaluated first, then Allow policies in order from top to bottom) means policy ordering in the application's policies list is informational only for this application's two-policy configuration. The team_domain crmbuilder.cloudflareaccess.com is visible to anyone who hits the OAuth flow at the no-auth path (path 1 verification observed it); this is normal Cloudflare Access behavior and is not a leak. The CRM Builder MCP application's session duration is set to Cloudflare's default 24 hours; this controls how long an admin browser session remains authenticated after PIN entry, not anything about the Service Token (which has no session in the conventional sense — each request is independently validated against the CF-Access-Client-Id + CF-Access-Client-Secret presented).
